news_metadata.title: Ford Inaugurates $2.5B EV Manufacturing Complex in Mexico, Secures Multi-Year Supply Deals
news_metadata.content:
RAMOS ARISPE, Mexico (AP) — Ford Motor Co. formally inaugurated its $2.5 billion electric vehicle manufacturing complex in northern Mexico on Wednesday, marking the automaker’s largest nearshoring investment in North America to date.
The 900-acre facility will produce next-generation electric pickup trucks and SUVs for the U.S. and Mexican markets, with an annual production capacity of 250,000 EVs. The plant features fully automated stamping and welding lines, fitted with 320 industrial robotic arms, and a dedicated battery assembly workshop.
Ford also announced 10-year supply agreements with global component manufacturers to stabilize its EV supply chain, including: $1.8 billion in orders for 1200V silicon carbide power modules from onsemi; $1.2 billion in lithium iron phosphate battery cells from CATL’s new Mexico plant; and $750 million in automotive-grade MCU chips from NXP Semiconductors.
Additional local supply contracts were awarded to Mexican manufacturers, including high-voltage automotive wiring harnesses from Lear Corp, and die-cast aluminum body structural components from Nemak. The company said the localized supply chain will cut cross-border logistics costs by 18% and reduce production lead times by 12 days.
Mexico’s economy ministry said the Ford complex will create 4,600 direct jobs and drive an additional 12,000 indirect jobs in the regional auto parts supply chain.
